commit.message:
fix: improve test coverage tracking and reporting (#903)

* fix: improve test coverage tracking and reporting

This commit addresses two key issues with test coverage in the AIGNE Framework:

## 1. Bun Coverage Gap Fix

Bun's test runner only tracks files that are imported during test execution,
leading to artificially high coverage percentages. This commit adds comprehensive
coverage tracking by force-loading all source files.

### Changes:
- Created `packages/test-utils/src/utils/import-all-modules.ts` utility
  - Uses Bun.Glob to discover all source files
  - Force-loads files via file:// URLs for reliable cross-CWD imports
  - Configurable exclusion patterns for test files and build artifacts

- Added `test/coverage.test.ts` to 26 packages
  - Forces all source files to be imported for coverage tracking
  - Ensures previously hidden files now appear in coverage reports

- Updated 27 package.json files with @aigne/test-utils devDependency

### Impact:
Previously invisible files (e.g., `src/loader/error.ts`) now correctly show 0%
coverage instead of being excluded from reports entirely.

## 2. Coverage Pollution Fix

Coverage reports included 195K+ lines of build artifacts and test infrastructure,
making it difficult to assess actual source code coverage.

### Changes:
- Created `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` post-processing script
  - Parses LCOV format and removes unwanted entries
  - Excludes: lib/*, dist/*, _mocks/*, _utils/*, test-agents/*, *.test.*, *.d.ts
  - Supports --all flag to process all lcov.info files in repository
  - Added --verbose flag for detailed output

- Created `bunfig.toml` with coveragePathIgnorePatterns
  - Documents intended exclusion patterns for future Bun versions
  - Note: These patterns don't work in Bun 1.3.x, hence the post-processing script

- Updated `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
  - Added clean coverage step before Codecov upload
  - Ensures only source code coverage is reported to Codecov

### Impact:
- Before: 230,135 lines tracked, 37.98% coverage (including build artifacts)
- After: 35,318 lines tracked, 61.18% coverage (source code only)
- Removed 1,724 unwanted entries from coverage reports

## Branch Coverage Limitation

Note: Bun test runner (as of v1.3.6) does not support branch coverage reporting.
Only line and function coverage are tracked. Branch coverage support is tracked
in Bun issue #7100 with no timeline for implementation.

If branch coverage is required, consider:
- Using c8 as a coverage wrapper
- Migrating to Vitest test runner

## Testing

All 26 packages verified with representative test runs:
- @aigne/core: 1,428 lines, 73.53% coverage
- @aigne/agent-library: 471 lines, 31.85% coverage
- @aigne/afs: 342 lines, 88.89% coverage
- @aigne/openai: 415 lines, 67.95% coverage
- @aigne/memory: 264 lines, 64.77% coverage

* fix: resolve cyclic workspace dependencies in coverage tracking

## Problem

The original implementation created cyclic dependencies:
- @aigne/core → @aigne/test-utils (devDep) → @aigne/core (dep) [CYCLE]
- @aigne/afs → @aigne/test-utils (devDep) → @aigne/core → @aigne/afs (dep) [CYCLE]
- @aigne/afs-history → @aigne/sqlite → @aigne/test-utils → @aigne/core → @aigne/afs-history [CYCLE]

This blocked pnpm install with cyclic dependency warnings.

## Solution

Moved the `importAllModules` utility from `@aigne/test-utils` package to monorepo root at `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts`.

This eliminates all cycles because:
- The utility is no longer part of any workspace package
- All packages can import it directly without creating dependencies
- No workspace dependencies are added for coverage tracking

## Changes

- Created `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts` - Shared coverage utility at monorepo root
- Removed `@aigne/test-utils` devDependency from:
  - `packages/core` (was in cycle via core)
  - `packages/platform-helpers` (was in cycle via core → platform-helpers)
  - `packages/sqlite` (was in cycle via afs-history → sqlite)
  - `afs/core` (was in cycle via core → afs)
  - `afs/history` (was in cycle via core → afs-history)
- Updated all 28 `coverage.test.ts` files to import from `scripts/test-utils/`

## Benefits

1. **No cycles**: pnpm install runs without warnings
2. **No duplication**: Single source of truth for coverage utility
3. **Simpler dependencies**: Packages that only need coverage tracking don't need test-utils devDependency
4. **Consistent**: All packages use the same utility from the same location

## Note

Packages that use `@aigne/test-utils` for other utilities (event-stream mocking, etc.) still keep it as a devDependency. The change only affects coverage tracking.

* fix: resolve TypeScript and test execution issues in coverage tracking

## Problem 1: TypeScript Import Error
TypeScript compiler rejects imports with .ts extensions:
```
error TS5097: An import path can only end with a '.ts' extension when
'allowImportingTsExtensions' is enabled.
```

## Problem 2: Incorrect Source Directory Resolution
Packages using `bun test` (instead of `bun --cwd test test`) were using
incorrect sourceDir="../src" which resolved to wrong paths.

For example, in packages/platform-helpers:
- Test runs from: packages/platform-helpers/
- Used "../src" which resolved to: packages/src (WRONG)
- Should be "./src" which resolves to: packages/platform-helpers/src (CORRECT)

## Solution

1. **Removed .ts extensions** from all import paths
   - Changed: `import { importAllModules } from "...import-all-modules.ts"`
   - To: `import { importAllModules } from "...import-all-modules"`
   - Both Bun and TypeScript handle extensionless imports correctly

2. **Fixed sourceDir for packages using `bun test`**
   - packages/platform-helpers: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/secrets: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/sqlite: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/transport: "../src" → "./src"

   Packages using `bun --cwd test test` still use "../src" (correct):
   - packages/core
   - packages/agent-library

## Verification

- ✅ `pnpm lint` passes for all packages
- ✅ `pnpm test` passes for all packages
- ✅ Coverage tracking works correctly across all 28 packages

* fix: resolve TypeScript/Biome linter conflict for .ts extensions

## Problem

Two linters had conflicting requirements for import paths:
- **TypeScript** (tsc): Rejected .ts extensions with error TS5097
- **Biome**: Required .ts extensions via lint/correctness/useImportExtensions

This created a deadlock where satisfying one linter broke the other.

## Root Cause

TypeScript by default doesn't allow .ts extensions in import paths unless
`allowImportingTsExtensions` is explicitly enabled. However, Biome (and modern
bundlers like Bun) prefer explicit extensions for better tooling and resolution.

## Solution

Enabled TypeScript's `allowImportingTsExtensions` compiler option in root tsconfig.json:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "allowImportingTsExtensions": true,  // Added
    ...
  }
}
```

This allows TypeScript to accept .ts extensions, making it compatible with Biome's
requirement. Since this is a Bun-based monorepo with `"moduleResolution": "bundler"`,
.ts extensions work correctly at runtime.

## Changes

- ✅ Updated `tsconfig.json` with `allowImportingTsExtensions: true`
- ✅ Added .ts extensions to all 28 coverage.test.ts imports
- ✅ Both TypeScript and Biome linting now pass
- ✅ All tests continue to work correctly

## Verification

```bash
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers lint  # ✅ TypeScript passes
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers test  # ✅ Tests pass
```

* fix: override allowImportingTsExtensions in build configs

## Problem

CI build failed with error TS5096:
```
Option 'allowImportingTsExtensions' can only be used when either
'noEmit' or 'emitDeclarationOnly' is set.
```

This occurred because build configs inherit from root tsconfig.json
(which has `allowImportingTsExtensions: true` for linting) but they
set `noEmit: false` to actually emit compiled files.

## Root Cause

TypeScript restriction: `allowImportingTsExtensions` is only allowed when:
- `noEmit: true` (no files emitted, only type checking), OR
- `emitDeclarationOnly: true` (only .d.ts files emitted)

Build configs need to emit actual JS files, so they can't use this option.

## Solution

Override `allowImportingTsExtensions: false` in all 28 build configs:
- `*/scripts/tsconfig.build.json`

Since test files (which use .ts extensions) aren't included in builds,
the build configs don't need this setting. It's only needed for linting
test files.

## Changes

Updated 28 build configs across:
- 7 packages (core, agent-library, platform-helpers, secrets, sqlite, transport)
- 17 models (openai, anthropic, gemini, etc.)
- 4 afs modules (core, history, local-fs, user-profile-memory)
- 4 memory modules (default, agentic, did-space, fs)
- 1 observability package (api)

## Verification

```bash
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers build  # ✅ Builds successfully
pnpm -F @aigne/core build              # ✅ Builds successfully
```

## Context

- Root tsconfig.json keeps `allowImportingTsExtensions: true` for test linting
- Build configs override to `false` since they emit files and don't include tests
- Tests continue to work with .ts extensions via Bun runtime

* fix: add missing allowImportingTsExtensions overrides

## Problem

Previous commit missed 4 build configs that also need the override:
- packages/test-utils/tsconfig.build.json
- packages/cli/tsconfig.build.json
- observability/blocklet/tsconfig.api.json
- blocklets/agent-runtime/tsconfig.api.json

These were in different locations (package root, not scripts/ subdir)
so weren't caught by the initial fix.

## Solution

Added `allowImportingTsExtensions: false` to all 4 configs.

## Verification

✅ Full monorepo build passes: `pnpm build`
✅ Core packages lint successfully:
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/core lint`
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers lint`
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/afs lint`

* fix: exclude scripts/ directory from coverage reports

## Problem

The cleanup script didn't exclude `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts`
and other utility scripts from coverage reports. These are build/test
infrastructure, not application source code, and shouldn't be tracked in coverage.

## Solution

Added `scripts/` directory to exclusion patterns in both:
- `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` - Post-processing script
- `bunfig.toml` - Bun test configuration (for future compatibility)

## Pattern Added

```javascript
// In clean-coverage.cjs
/(^|\/)scripts\//,

// In bunfig.toml
"**/scripts/**",
```

This ensures:
- ✅ `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts` excluded
- ✅ `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` excluded
- ✅ `scripts/post-build-lib.mjs` excluded
- ✅ Package-level `scripts/tsconfig.build.json` files excluded

## Verification

Scripts directory contains build/test utilities only, not source code:
- `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` - Coverage post-processor
- `scripts/test-utils/` - Test utilities
- `scripts/post-build-lib.mjs` - Build post-processor
- `packages/*/scripts/` - Package build configs

  - `packages/platform-helpers` (was in cycle via core → platform-helpers)
  - `packages/sqlite` (was in cycle via afs-history → sqlite)
  - `afs/core` (was in cycle via core → afs)
  - `afs/history` (was in cycle via core → afs-history)
- Updated all 28 `coverage.test.ts` files to import from `scripts/test-utils/`

## Benefits

1. **No cycles**: pnpm install runs without warnings
2. **No duplication**: Single source of truth for coverage utility
3. **Simpler dependencies**: Packages that only need coverage tracking don't need test-utils devDependency
4. **Consistent**: All packages use the same utility from the same location

## Note

Packages that use `@aigne/test-utils` for other utilities (event-stream mocking, etc.) still keep it as a devDependency. The change only affects coverage tracking.

* fix: resolve TypeScript and test execution issues in coverage tracking

## Problem 1: TypeScript Import Error
TypeScript compiler rejects imports with .ts extensions:
```
error TS5097: An import path can only end with a '.ts' extension when
'allowImportingTsExtensions' is enabled.
```

## Problem 2: Incorrect Source Directory Resolution
Packages using `bun test` (instead of `bun --cwd test test`) were using
incorrect sourceDir="../src" which resolved to wrong paths.

For example, in packages/platform-helpers:
- Test runs from: packages/platform-helpers/
- Used "../src" which resolved to: packages/src (WRONG)
- Should be "./src" which resolves to: packages/platform-helpers/src (CORRECT)

## Solution

1. **Removed .ts extensions** from all import paths
   - Changed: `import { importAllModules } from "...import-all-modules.ts"`
   - To: `import { importAllModules } from "...import-all-modules"`
   - Both Bun and TypeScript handle extensionless imports correctly

2. **Fixed sourceDir for packages using `bun test`**
   - packages/platform-helpers: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/secrets: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/sqlite: "../src" → "./src"
   - packages/transport: "../src" → "./src"

   Packages using `bun --cwd test test` still use "../src" (correct):
   - packages/core
   - packages/agent-library

## Verification

- ✅ `pnpm lint` passes for all packages
- ✅ `pnpm test` passes for all packages
- ✅ Coverage tracking works correctly across all 28 packages

* fix: resolve TypeScript/Biome linter conflict for .ts extensions

## Problem

Two linters had conflicting requirements for import paths:
- **TypeScript** (tsc): Rejected .ts extensions with error TS5097
- **Biome**: Required .ts extensions via lint/correctness/useImportExtensions

This created a deadlock where satisfying one linter broke the other.

## Root Cause

TypeScript by default doesn't allow .ts extensions in import paths unless
`allowImportingTsExtensions` is explicitly enabled. However, Biome (and modern
bundlers like Bun) prefer explicit extensions for better tooling and resolution.

## Solution

Enabled TypeScript's `allowImportingTsExtensions` compiler option in root tsconfig.json:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "allowImportingTsExtensions": true,  // Added
    ...
  }
}
```

This allows TypeScript to accept .ts extensions, making it compatible with Biome's
requirement. Since this is a Bun-based monorepo with `"moduleResolution": "bundler"`,
.ts extensions work correctly at runtime.

## Changes

- ✅ Updated `tsconfig.json` with `allowImportingTsExtensions: true`
- ✅ Added .ts extensions to all 28 coverage.test.ts imports
- ✅ Both TypeScript and Biome linting now pass
- ✅ All tests continue to work correctly

## Verification

```bash
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers lint  # ✅ TypeScript passes
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers test  # ✅ Tests pass
```

* fix: override allowImportingTsExtensions in build configs

## Problem

CI build failed with error TS5096:
```
Option 'allowImportingTsExtensions' can only be used when either
'noEmit' or 'emitDeclarationOnly' is set.
```

This occurred because build configs inherit from root tsconfig.json
(which has `allowImportingTsExtensions: true` for linting) but they
set `noEmit: false` to actually emit compiled files.

## Root Cause

TypeScript restriction: `allowImportingTsExtensions` is only allowed when:
- `noEmit: true` (no files emitted, only type checking), OR
- `emitDeclarationOnly: true` (only .d.ts files emitted)

Build configs need to emit actual JS files, so they can't use this option.

## Solution

Override `allowImportingTsExtensions: false` in all 28 build configs:
- `*/scripts/tsconfig.build.json`

Since test files (which use .ts extensions) aren't included in builds,
the build configs don't need this setting. It's only needed for linting
test files.

## Changes

Updated 28 build configs across:
- 7 packages (core, agent-library, platform-helpers, secrets, sqlite, transport)
- 17 models (openai, anthropic, gemini, etc.)
- 4 afs modules (core, history, local-fs, user-profile-memory)
- 4 memory modules (default, agentic, did-space, fs)
- 1 observability package (api)

## Verification

```bash
pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers build  # ✅ Builds successfully
pnpm -F @aigne/core build              # ✅ Builds successfully
```

## Context

- Root tsconfig.json keeps `allowImportingTsExtensions: true` for test linting
- Build configs override to `false` since they emit files and don't include tests
- Tests continue to work with .ts extensions via Bun runtime

* fix: add missing allowImportingTsExtensions overrides

## Problem

Previous commit missed 4 build configs that also need the override:
- packages/test-utils/tsconfig.build.json
- packages/cli/tsconfig.build.json
- observability/blocklet/tsconfig.api.json
- blocklets/agent-runtime/tsconfig.api.json

These were in different locations (package root, not scripts/ subdir)
so weren't caught by the initial fix.

## Solution

Added `allowImportingTsExtensions: false` to all 4 configs.

## Verification

✅ Full monorepo build passes: `pnpm build`
✅ Core packages lint successfully:
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/core lint`
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/platform-helpers lint`
   - `pnpm -F @aigne/afs lint`

* fix: exclude scripts/ directory from coverage reports

## Problem

The cleanup script didn't exclude `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts`
and other utility scripts from coverage reports. These are build/test
infrastructure, not application source code, and shouldn't be tracked in coverage.

## Solution

Added `scripts/` directory to exclusion patterns in both:
- `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` - Post-processing script
- `bunfig.toml` - Bun test configuration (for future compatibility)

## Pattern Added

```javascript
// In clean-coverage.cjs
/(^|\/)scripts\//,

// In bunfig.toml
"**/scripts/**",
```

This ensures:
- ✅ `scripts/test-utils/import-all-modules.ts` excluded
- ✅ `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` excluded
- ✅ `scripts/post-build-lib.mjs` excluded
- ✅ Package-level `scripts/tsconfig.build.json` files excluded

## Verification

Scripts directory contains build/test utilities only, not source code:
- `scripts/clean-coverage.cjs` - Coverage post-processor
- `scripts/test-utils/` - Test utilities
- `scripts/post-build-lib.mjs` - Build post-processor
- `packages/*/scripts/` - Package build configs

readme_html:
What is AIGNE Framework

AIGNE Framework [ ˈei dʒən ] is a functional AI application development framework designed to simplify and accelerate the process of building modern applications. It combines functional programming features, powerful artificial intelligence capabilities, and modular design principles to help developers easily create scalable solutions. AIGNE Framework is also deeply integrated with the Blocklet ecosystem, providing developers with a wealth of tools and resources.

Key Features

  • Modular Design: With a clear modular structure, developers can easily organize code, improve development efficiency, and simplify maintenance.
  • TypeScript Support: Comprehensive TypeScript type definitions are provided, ensuring type safety and enhancing the developer experience.
  • Multiple AI Model Support: Built-in support for OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, Nova and other mainstream AI models, easily extensible to support additional models.
  • Flexible Workflow Patterns: Support for sequential, concurrent, routing, handoff and other workflow patterns to meet various complex application requirements.
  • Agentic File System (AFS): A virtual file system abstraction that provides AI agents with unified access to various storage backends, including local files, conversation history, and user profiles. See AFS Documentation for details.
  • MCP Protocol Integration: Seamless integration with external systems and services through the Model Context Protocol.
  • Code Execution Capabilities: Support for executing dynamically generated code in a secure sandbox, enabling more powerful automation capabilities.
  • Blocklet Ecosystem Integration: Closely integrated with ArcBlock's Blocklet ecosystem, providing developers with a one-stop solution for development and deployment.

Quick Start

Environment Requirements

  • Node.js version 20.0 or higher

Installation

Using npm

npm install @aigne/core

Using yarn

yarn add @aigne/core

Using pnpm

pnpm add @aigne/core

Usage Example

import { AIAgent, AIGNE } from "@aigne/core";
import { OpenAIChatModel } from "@aigne/openai";

const { OPENAI_API_KEY } = process.env;

const model = new OpenAIChatModel({
  apiKey: OPENAI_API_KEY,
});

function transferToB() {
  return agentB;
}

const agentA = AIAgent.from({
  name: "AgentA",
  instructions: "You are a helpful agent.",
  outputKey: "A",
  skills: [transferToB],
  inputKey: "message",
});

const agentB = AIAgent.from({
  name: "AgentB",
  instructions: "Only speak in Haikus.",
  outputKey: "B",
  inputKey: "message",
});

const aigne = new AIGNE({ model });

const userAgent = aigne.invoke(agentA);

const result1 = await userAgent.invoke({ message: "transfer to agent b" });
console.log(result1);
// Output:
// {
//   B: "Transfer now complete,  \nAgent B is here to help.  \nWhat do you need, friend?",
// }

const result2 = await userAgent.invoke({ message: "It's a beautiful day" });
console.log(result2);
// Output:
// {
//   B: "Sunshine warms the earth,  \nGentle breeze whispers softly,  \nNature sings with joy.  ",
// }

Architecture diagram

Simplifying Agentic Workflows

The AIGNE Framework offers multiple workflow patterns, each tailored to address distinct application scenarios efficiently.

Workflow Patterns

  • Workflow Router - Implement intelligent routing logic to direct requests to appropriate handlers based on content.
  • Workflow Sequential - Build step-by-step processing pipelines with guaranteed execution order.
  • Workflow Concurrency - Optimize performance by processing multiple tasks simultaneously with parallel execution.
  • Workflow Handoff - Create seamless transitions between specialized agents to solve complex problems.
  • Workflow Reflection - Enable self-improvement through output evaluation and refinement capabilities.
  • Workflow Orchestration - Coordinate multiple agents working together in sophisticated processing pipelines.
  • Workflow Code Execution - Safely execute dynamically generated code within AI-driven workflows.
  • Workflow Group Chat - Share messages and interact with multiple agents in a group chat environment.

Sequential

Use Cases: Processing multi-step tasks that require a specific execution order, such as content generation pipelines, multi-stage data processing, etc.

Agentic File System (AFS)

AFS provides AI agents with a unified interface to access various storage backends through a virtual file system abstraction. This enables agents to work with local files, conversation history, and user profiles using consistent APIs.

Features

  • Virtual File System: Path-based access to diverse data sources
  • Pluggable Modules: Easily extend with custom storage backends
  • Automatic Tool Registration: AI agents automatically get file system capabilities
  • Persistent Storage: SQLite-based storage with automatic migrations
  • Conversation Memory: Built-in history tracking and user profile management

AFS Examples

  • AFS Memory Example - Explore conversation memory and user profile management capabilities.
  • AFS LocalFS Example - Learn how to mount local file systems and let AI agents interact with your files through natural language.
  • AFS MCP Server Example - Mount MCP servers as AFS modules to integrate external services like GitHub.

For comprehensive AFS documentation, see AFS Documentation.

Built-in MCP Support

Built-in MCP support allows the AIGNE framework to effortlessly run its own MCP server or seamlessly integrate with external MCP servers.

Implement MCP Server

  • MCP Server - Build a MCP server using AIGNE CLI to provide MCP services.

Use MCP Servers

  • Puppeteer MCP Server - Learn how to leverage Puppeteer for automated web scraping through the AIGNE Framework.
  • SQLite MCP Server - Explore database operations by connecting to SQLite through the Model Context Protocol.
  • Github - Interact with GitHub repositories using the GitHub MCP Server.

Packages

  • examples - Example project demonstrating how to use different agents to handle various tasks.
  • packages/core - Core package providing the foundation for building AIGNE applications.
  • packages/agent-library - AIGNE agent library, providing a variety of specialized agents for different tasks.
  • packages/cli - Command-line interface for AIGNE Framework, providing tools for project management and deployment.
  • afs - Agentic File System (AFS), providing virtual file system abstraction for AI agents.
    • afs/core - Core AFS implementation with history module.
    • afs/local-fs - Local file system module for AFS.
    • afs/user-profile-memory - User profile memory module for AFS.
  • models - AIGNE Framework's built-in models, including OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, and Nova.
    • models/openai - OpenAI model implementation, supporting OpenAI's API and function calling.
    • models/anthropic - Anthropic model implementation, supporting Anthropic's API and function calling.
    • models/bedrock - Bedrock model implementation, supporting Bedrock's API and function calling.
    • models/deepseek - DeepSeek model implementation, supporting DeepSeek's API and function calling.
    • models/gemini - Gemini model implementation, supporting Gemini's API and function calling.
    • models/ollama - Ollama model implementation, supporting Ollama's API and function calling.
    • models/open-router - OpenRouter model implementation, supporting OpenRouter's API and function calling.
    • models/xai - XAI model implementation, supporting XAI's API and function calling.

Documentation

AIGNE Framework Documentation provides comprehensive guides and API references to help developers quickly get started and master the framework.

Contributing and Releasing

AIGNE Framework is an open source project and welcomes community contributions. We use release-please for version management and release automation.

  • Contributing Guidelines: See CONTRIBUTING.md
  • Release Process: See RELEASING.md

About the Name

📛 AIGNE Name & Meaning

[ ˈei dʒən ] — like "agent" without the t

🔤 Pronunciation

It's pronounced [ ˈei dʒən ] — like "agent" without the "t."

🏞️ Origin

Aigne is a small medieval village in southern France.

In Old Irish, aigne means "spirit" — a perfect metaphor for agents that think and act.

🔡 Acronym

AIGNE = AI Genesis Native Engineering.

An open engineering paradigm for systems born with AI-native rules and brought to life through autonomous agents.

Community and Support

AIGNE Framework has a vibrant developer community offering various support channels:

  • Documentation Center: Comprehensive official documentation to help developers get started quickly.
  • Technical Forum: Exchange experiences with global developers and solve technical problems.
